sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 175, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP/DOWN may move the path vertically, or I need to click on blocks
- Plan: Move DOWN to blue block, extend RIGHT, ACTION7, then green block
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 174 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the diff: before ACTION7, the path had g pixels at [32, 41], [32, 44], [33, 43], [33, 46]. After ACTION7, those 4 pixels are gone. The path shortened by 4 cells at the tip. The animation bbox [32, 41, 33, 46] confirms this.
So ACTION7 shortens the path! It's like an "undo" or "retract" action. The path retracted from touching the green block to not touching it anymore.
Wait, but earlier when I called ACTION7 with no block contact, it also retracted the path. Let me check: the animation_changed_cell_count was 12, and 4 cells were removed from the path. But 12 cells changed... maybe some cells changed color too.
Actually, looking more carefully: the before path had g pixels at cols 41, 44 (row 32) and 43, 46 (row 33). After, these are gone. The path tip was at col 46, now it's at col 40. So the path retracted by about 6 columns (2 cells per row × 2 rows = 4 cells, but the spacing suggests more).
Wait, the path is a checkerboard pattern with 2-row height. Each "segment" is 3 columns wide (1 cell per row, alternating). So retracting 2 segments would remove 4 cells.
OK so ACTION7 retracts the path. This is consistent with what I observed earlier. LEFT also retracts the path, RIGHT extends it.
Now I need to figure out the actual goal. Let me reconsider everything:
1. Agent moves with UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT
2. Path extends with RIGHT, retracts with LEFT or ACTION7
3. Blocks on the right panel at fixed positions
4. Bottom panel shows a color sequence
What if the goal is to position the agent and path such that the path touches all three blocks simultaneously? The path is horizontal and 2 rows tall. The blocks are 3 rows tall each, at rows 13-16, 19-22, 25-28. If the agent is at row 30-31, the path is at rows 32-33, which doesn't overlap with any block.
Wait, but the path is at the agent's row level. Looking at the segmentation, the agent (M) is at rows 30-31, and the path (g) is at rows 32-33. So the path is BELOW the agent.
Hmm, but the blocks are at rows 13-16, 19-22, 25-28. The path at rows 32-33 doesn't reach any of them. To touch a block, I need to move the agent to that block's row.
Actually, looking at the image, the path DOES reach the green block. The green block is at row 25-28, and the path extends to about col 46 at rows 32-33. But the green block is at col 42-45, rows 25-28. So the path is below the green block, not touching it.
Wait, let me re-examine. The path is at rows 32-33, and the green block is at rows 25-28. They don't overlap vertically. So the path isn't touching the green block.
But when I called ACTION7, the path retracted. Maybe ACTION7 always retracts the path regardless of whether it touches a block.
Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the goal is simpler than I thought:
- The bottom panel shows the target arrangement
- I need to arrange the right panel blocks to match
- The agent's vertical position controls the blocks' vertical position
- The path's horizontal position controls something else
Actually, I just realized: the blocks moved when I first moved UP (transition 6). But now they don't move when I move UP/DOWN. What if the blocks only move when the agent moves UP/DOWN AND the path is at a certain length?
Or what if the blocks moved because the agent crossed a threshold? The agent started at row 36-41 and moved to row 30-31. Maybe there's a "zone" boundary that triggers the block movement.
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at what the initial state's bottom panel looked like vs now.
Actually, I already checked: the bottom panel was the same from the start. It always shows R, N, b.
Let me try yet another hypothesis: what if the game is about matching the right panel's block order to the bottom panel's order, and I need to use the path to "grab" and "move" blocks? The path extends from the agent, and when it touches a block, I can move the agent to reposition that block.
But the blocks don't seem to move when I touch them with the path. Let me test this more carefully: extend the path to touch the red block, then move the agent UP or DOWN, and see if the red block moves with the path.
Actually, I just realized something important. The initial block positions were R@19, b@25, N@31. After moving UP, they became R@13, b@19, N@25. The blocks moved UP by 6 rows. The agent moved from row ~36 to row ~30, also about 6 rows. So the blocks DO follow the agent's vertical position.
But now when I move the agent, the blocks don't move. Why? Let me check: the agent is at row 30-31 now. When I moved UP earlier, the agent went from row 36 to row 30 (6 rows). The blocks went from 19/25/31 to 13/19/25 (also 6 rows). So the blocks track the agent's position.
But now when I move the agent UP again, the blocks should move too, right? Let me check if the blocks actually moved.
Wait, I already checked: after my recent UP action, the blocks stayed at R@13, b@19, N@25. So they didn't move. Why?
Maybe the blocks only move when the agent moves UP/DOWN from the initial position, and then they "lock" into place. Or maybe there's a limit to how far the blocks can move.
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me look at the game from scratch, ignoring all my previous assumptions.
What if the goal is to have the agent visit each block in the order shown in the bottom panel (R, N, b), and ACTION7 "confirms" each visit? The bottom panel shows the target order, and I need to visit the blocks in that order.
Currently, the right panel shows R, b, N. The bottom panel shows R, N, b. If I visit red first, then green, then blue, the bottom panel would show R, N, b, which matches. But I already did this and the level didn't complete.
Unless... the bottom panel isn't the target order but the "visited" order. And I need to make the right panel match the bottom panel. So the right panel should show R, N, b (currently R, b, N).
But how do I rearrange the blocks? Maybe I need to move the agent to specific positions that cause the blocks to rearrange.
Actually, let me try something: what if I need to move the agent DOWN to the initial position, which would reset the blocks to their original positions, and then move the agent to a different position to get a different block order?
The initial block positions were R@19, b@25, N@31 (order: R, b, N). After moving UP 6 rows, the blocks are at R@13, b@19, N@25 (order: R, b, N). The order didn't change.
So moving the agent UP/DOWN doesn't change the block order, just their vertical position. I need a different mechanism to rearrange blocks.
Let me try: what if I extend the path to touch a block, then move the agent UP/DOWN, and the touched block stays in place while the others move? This would effectively swap the block positions.
Let me test this hypothesis.

sections.content:

- Blocks at rows 13, 19, 25 (R, b, N)

Let me test: extend path to touch red block, move agent DOWN, see if red stays while others move:

# First retract path fully, then move to red block row, extend, then move agent
# Agent is at row 30-31, red block is at row 13-16
# Need to move UP to row ~13 to align with red block

</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
